---
title: "HW 08: Simpson's Paradox"
---

| | |
|---|---|
| **Type** | Computational |
| **Due** | Week 11 |
| **Weight** | Part of Homework (50% of final grade) |
| **Grading** | Completion-based |
| **Dataset** | `berkeley` from [`regdatasets`]({{< var books.regdatasets >}}){target="_blank"} |
| **Related Reading** | [Ch 09: Simple Logistic Regression]({{< var books.lecture_notes >}}/chapters/09-simple-logistic-regression.html){target="_blank"}, [Ch 10: Multiple Logistic Regression]({{< var books.lecture_notes >}}/chapters/10-multiple-logistic-regression.html){target="_blank"} |
| **Related Lab** | [Lab 10: Logistic Extended]({{< var books.r_lab_book >}}/labs/lab10-logistic-extended.html){target="_blank"} |

::: {.callout-note}
## Grading Policy
Homework is graded on **completion**. Submit a good-faith effort for full credit. A good-faith effort means: (1) all problems are attempted, (2) for computational assignments, R code runs without errors, and (3) written interpretations are included where requested.
:::

## Setup

This assignment uses the `berkeley` dataset from the `regdatasets` package, which contains data on graduate admissions at UC Berkeley. The variables include admission status (`admitted`), applicant gender (`female`), and department (`department`). You will investigate **Simpson's Paradox** --- a phenomenon where a trend that appears in aggregated data reverses when the data are disaggregated by a confounding variable.

## Problems

### Problem 1

Construct a **three-way frequency table** of admission status by gender by department. Display the counts of admitted and rejected applicants for each gender within each department.

### Problem 2

Collapse the table from Problem 1 over departments to create a **two-way table** of admission status by gender. Calculate the **odds ratio** comparing the odds of admission for male versus female applicants. Interpret the result.

### Problem 3

Fit a **logistic regression** model predicting admission status from `female` and `department` (i.e., controlling for department). Report the **odds ratios** for all predictors and interpret the odds ratio for `female`.

### Problem 4

Compare the odds ratio for `female` from Problem 3 (adjusted for department) to the odds ratio from Problem 2 (unadjusted). How and why do they differ? Explain this as an example of **Simpson's Paradox**, identifying the role of department as a confounding variable.

### Problem 5

Conduct a **Wald test** for the coefficient of `female` in the logistic regression model from Problem 3. State the null and alternative hypotheses, report the test statistic and $p$-value, and state your conclusion.

### Problem 6

Assess the **goodness of fit** of the logistic regression model from Problem 3. Use an appropriate test or diagnostic measure, report the results, and discuss whether the model fits the data adequately.


## Submission

1. Download the starter `.qmd` file and data from Blackboard.
2. Complete the analysis in R, filling in the code chunks and written responses.
3. Render your Quarto document to **PDF** and submit to Blackboard by the due date.
